questions.text: Study the four statements (A-D) given below and select the two correct ones out of them. A. The definition of biological species was given by Ernst Mayr. B. Photoperiod does not affect reproduction in plants.C. The binomial nomenclature system was given by R.H. Whittaker. D. In unicellular organisms, reproduction is synonymous with growth.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Photoperiod affects flowering and reproduction in plants.Binomial nomenclature system was given by Carolus Linnaeus.
D is correct, but C is wrong since binomial nomenclature was proposed by Linnaeus, not Whittaker.
Both are true. Ernst Mayr gave the biological species concept, and in unicellular organisms growth and reproduction are synonymous.
While A is correct, B is wrong because photoperiod strongly influences reproduction in plants, especially flowering.

questions.explanation: Among the given statements, A is correct because Ernst Mayr proposed the widely accepted biological species concept, which defines a species as a group of interbreeding organisms that are reproductively isolated from others. B is incorrect since photoperiod, or the duration of light and dark, plays a crucial role in regulating flowering and reproduction in plants, with many species classified as short-day, long-day, or day-neutral. C is also incorrect because the system of binomial nomenclature was not given by R.H. Whittaker but by Carl Linnaeus. At the same time, Whittaker is known for the five-kingdom system of classification. D is correct, as in unicellular organisms like amoeba and bacteria, growth and reproduction are synonymous processes; cell growth directly leads to cell division and multiplication. Therefore, the two correct statements are A and D, while B and C are contrary to the rules and concepts of biology.

questions.text: Nomenclature is governed by certain universal rules. Which one of the following statements is correct according to these rules?
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Both the genus name and species name begin with capital letters.
options.explanation: Incorrect. Only the genus name is capitalized, while the species name always starts with a lowercase letter. For example, the correct way is Panthera tigris, not Panthera Tigris.

Incorrect. Scientific names are always written in Latin or Latinized form because Latin is a “dead language” and does not change over time. Using local languages would create confusion and inconsistency worldwide.

options.text: The generic name always starts with a capital letter, while the species name starts with a small letter.
options.explanation: This is the fundamental rule of binomial nomenclature. For example, Homo sapiens—“Homo” (genus) starts with a capital letter, while “sapiens” (species) starts with a small letter.

options.text: The author’s name should always be written in full (never abbreviated) after the scientific name.
options.explanation: Incorrect. The author’s name is written in abbreviated form, not in full. For example, Mangifera indica L. (“L.” stands for Linnaeus). Writing the full name is not standard practice.

questions.explanation: The correct option is C because the genus name begins with a capital letter and the species name with a lowercase letter. Options A, B, and D are contrary to the rules of nomenclature.

questions.text: The term phylum was given by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Factually incorrect.
This option is correct because, The term phylum was coined in 1866 by Ernst Haeckel from the Greek phylon (φῦλον, "race, stock"), related to phyle (φυλή, "tribe, clan").
Factually incorrect.
Factually incorrect.

questions.explanation: The term phylum was coined in 1866 by Ernst Haeckel from the Greek phylon (φῦλον, "race, stock"), related to phyle (φυλή, "tribe, clan"). The concept of "phylum" was actually developed by the German biologist Ernst Haeckel (1834-1919) in the 19th century. Haeckel is credited with proposing the term "phylum" as a rank in the hierarchy of biological classification to group organisms with similar body plans and organizational features.

questions.text: A group of plants or animals with similar traits of any rank is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the Lowest taxonomic rank is specie. A species is a group of organisms that have numerous physical features in common and which are capable of interbreeding and producing offspring.
Factually Incorrect.
This option is incorrect because families are grouped into order.

A taxon (plural taxa) or taxonomic unit, is a name designating an organism or group of organisms. A taxon is assigned a rank and can be placed at a particular level in a systematic hierarchy reflecting evolutionary relationships.

questions.explanation: A taxon (plural taxa) or taxonomic unit, is a name designating an organism or group of organisms. A taxon is assigned a rank and can be placed at a particular level in a systematic hierarchy reflecting evolutionary relationships.

questions.text: A taxon is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because a group of related families is not a taxon.
This option is incorrect because a group of related species is called genera.
This option is incorrect because a taxon is not a type of living organism.

options.text: A taxonomic group of any ranking
options.explanation: The word taxon signifies a taxonomic group of any rank that represents the real biological organisms included in a category. The term taxon was coined by Adolf Meyer (1926) for animals and H.J Lam (1948) used this term in plant science.

questions.explanation: The word taxon signifies a taxonomic group of any rank that represents the real biological organisms included in a category. The term taxon was coined by Adolf Meyer (1926) for animals and H.J Lam (1948) used this term in plant science. STB page # 82

questions.text: Which of the following sets of diseases is caused by bacteria?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because both diseases are caused by bacteria. Cholera is caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae whereas tetanus is caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani.
This option is incorrect because typhoid is caused by the bacterium Salmonella typhi whereas smallpox is caused by Variola virus.
This option is incorrect because Tetanus is caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani while mumps is caused by a virus(paramyxovirus).
This option is incorrect because Herpes is caused by herpes simplex virus and influenza is caused by a virus(Orthomyxovirus).

questions.explanation: Among all options, A is the correct one because both diseases are caused by bacteria. Cholera is caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae whereas tetanus is caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani. All other options contain errors.

questions.text: One of the most important functions of botanical gardens is that:
questions.subject: Biology
questions.topic: Biology and its Major Fields of Specialization

options.text: They provide a beautiful area for recreation
options.explanation: Botanical gardens do provide a beautiful area for recreation, but that is not their most important function because their primary role is scientific and educational, not leisure.

options.text: One can observe tropical plants there
options.explanation: Showcasing tropical plants is a visible feature, but it is secondary. The core purpose is conservation, research, and education.

options.text: They allow ex-situ conservation of germplasm
options.explanation: Ex-situ conservation means “off-site conservation”. It is the process of protecting endangered species of plants and animals by removing them from an unsafe or threatened habitat and placing them or part of them under the care of humans. Botanical gardens serve as ex-situ conservation of germplasm of different plants, to maintain rare and endemic plant species, and also to provide recreation and knowledge about plants to the common man.

options.text: They provide a natural habitat for wildlife
options.explanation: Providing natural habitat for wildlife is secondary/accidental, but the important function is conserving and studying plant diversity.

questions.explanation: Ex-situ conservation means “off-site conservation.” It is the process of protecting endangered species of plants and animals by removing them from an unsafe or threatened habitat and placing them or part of them under the care of humans. Botanical gardens serve as ex-situ conservation of germplasm of different plants, to maintain rare and endemic plant species, and also to provide recreation and knowledge about plants to the common man.

questions.text: Widal test is carried out to check:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because The Widal test is a blood test used to diagnose or confirm a suspected case of enteric fever, including typhoid and paratyphoid fever.
This option is incorrect because The Widal test is a blood test used to diagnose or confirm a suspected case of enteric fever, including typhoid and paratyphoid fever.
This option is incorrect because The Widal test is a blood test used to diagnose or confirm a suspected case of enteric fever, including typhoid and paratyphoid fever.
This option is correct because The Widal test is a blood test used to diagnose or confirm a suspected case of enteric fever, including typhoid and paratyphoid fever.

questions.explanation: Widal test (developed by G.F.I Widal) is an agglutination test for the presence of antibodies against the Salmonella organism that causes typhoid fever. It is used to diagnose the presence of the disease in a patient. The Widal test works by detecting antibodies produced by the immune system in response to the Salmonella bacteria. It is not typically used as a first-line diagnostic test but may be employed when enteric fever is strongly suspected or during outbreaks.

questions.text: The sequence of taxonomic categories is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because phylum comes before class.
This option is incorrect because the order comes after the class.

This option is correct because, to construct the hierarchy of classification, one or more species are grouped into a genus, one or more genera into a family, families are clubbed into an order, orders into a class, classes into a phylum, and various phyla into a kingdom.
This option is incorrect because genus comes after family.

questions.explanation: According to biosystematics, to construct the hierarchy of classification, one or more species are grouped into a genus, one or more genera into a family, families are clubbed into an order, orders into a class, classes into a phylum, and various phyla into a kingdom.

questions.text: The common cold is not cured by antibiotics because it is:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is correct because the common cold is not cured by antibiotics because it is primarily caused by viruses, not bacteria. Antibiotics are effective against bacterial infections but do not work against viral infections.

options.text: Caused by a Gram positive bacterium
options.explanation: This option is correct because the common cold is not caused by a gram-positive bacterium; it is caused by a virus.

options.text: Caused by a Gram negative bacterium
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because the common cold is not a bacterial disease.
This option is incorrect because the common cold is an infectious disease and not cured by antibiotics, as it is primarily caused by viruses, not bacteria.

questions.explanation: The common cold is caused by some 100 types of rhinoviruses. It is one of the most common infectious diseases in humans. Antibiotics are substances that destroy or inhibit the growth of microorganisms, particularly disease-producing bacteria and fungi. Antibiotics are obtained from microorganisms (especially molds) or synthesised. Many antibiotics interfere with the pathogen's protein synthesis. Some (e.g., penicillin) prevent cross-linking of the glycan chains of peptidoglycan molecules in bacterial cell walls. Since the viruses do not possess cell walls or their own protein-synthesizing apparatus, antibiotics do not attack them.

questions.text: Which is the most infectious disease?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Hepatitis B is highly infectious because it can be transmitted through various bodily fluids, including blood, semen, vaginal fluids, and saliva.

AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome), caused by HIV (Human Immunodeficiency Virus), is one of the most infectious diseases. It is transmitted through various means, including unprotected sexual contact, sharing needles, and from mother to child during childbirth or breastfeeding.
The common cold and cough are infectious, but they are generally less severe and easier to contain compared to AIDS. They spread through respiratory droplets and direct contact.

Malaria is caused by the Plasmodium parasite and is transmitted through the bite of infected mosquitoes. While it is a significant infectious disease, AIDS is typically considered more infectious due to its modes of transmission and impact on the immune system.

questions.explanation: Hepatitis B (serum hepatitis) can occur at any age, and its mode of transmission is through contact with infected blood. Infection is severe, often fatal, and is accompanied by symptoms such as loss of appetite, nausea, pale or whitish stool (due to a lack of bile), and jaundice. Even a very small amount, as little as 0.0002% of hepatitis B-infected blood contact, is sufficient to transmit hepatitis B.

questions.text: Typhoid fever is caused by :
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Shigella is a different bacterium that causes Shigellosis, a gastrointestinal infection with symptoms different from those of typhoid fever.
Escherichia coli (E. coli) is a different bacterium and can cause various gastrointestinal illnesses but is not the primary cause of typhoid fever.
Giardia is a parasite that causes Giardiasis, which is a gastrointestinal infection distinct from typhoid fever.
Typhoid fever is caused by Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi.

questions.explanation: Typhoid is caused by Salmonella typhi. The organisms of the disease are present in the stool, and they may also be found in urine. Therefore, they can be carried by water and contaminated food, which can lead to severe epidemics. Polio is caused by Enterovirus. TB is caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. Tetanus is caused by Clostridium tetani.

questions.text: The basic unit, or smallest taxon, of taxonomy/classification is
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: The basic unit or smallest taxon of taxonomy/classification is species. Species are groups of organisms that have numerous physical features in common and are capable of interbreeding and producing fertile offspring.
This option is incorrect because the kingdom is the highest taxon.
This option is incorrect because closely related genera are grouped into families, not species.
An order groups together related families, making it an even higher rank in taxonomy. It is used for broader classification, not for identifying individual organisms.

questions.explanation: The basic unit or smallest taxon of taxonomy/classification is species. Species are groups of organisms that have numerous physical features in common and are capable of interbreeding and producing fertile offspring. Taxonomists use species as the starting point for organizing and categorizing living organisms into broader hierarchical groups. The hierarchy of biological classification, from most specific to most general.

questions.text: Linnaeus developed a system of nomenclature called
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Monomial nomenclature refers to naming organisms with just a single word, often used in very early classification systems. However, this method lacked precision and could not distinguish closely related species. Linnaeus did not use this system.

Trinomial nomenclature is used in some cases to describe subspecies (three-part names), but it was not introduced by Linnaeus. Instead, it developed later as an extension of the binomial system to account for more detailed classification.

Linnaeus introduced the system of binomial nomenclature, which is still in use today. In this system, each organism is given a two-part scientific name: the first part is the genus name (always capitalized), and the second is the species name (written in lowercase). For example, Homo sapiens is the scientific name of humans. This system brought uniformity and universality to naming organisms, avoiding confusion caused by local or common names.

Before Linnaeus, organisms were sometimes described with long Latin names consisting of many words (polynomial nomenclature). Linnaeus rejected this system because it was complicated and impractical, replacing it with the simpler binomial system.

questions.explanation: Binomial nomenclature of scientific naming was first given by C. Linnaeus (1735) in his book Systema Naturae and later in “Species Plantarum” (1753). He used two Latin words for any organism, the first being a generic name and the second being a specific name. The generic name begins with a capital letter and the species name with a small letter.

questions.text: Ringworm in humans is caused by:
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This option is incorrect because ringworm in humans is caused by a group of fungi known as dermatophytes. These fungi can infect the skin, hair, and nails, leading to the development of ringworm, also known as dermatophytosis.

This option is correct because, Ringworm in humans is caused by a group of fungi known as dermatophytes. These fungi can infect the skin, hair, and nails, leading to the development of ringworm, also known as dermatophytosis.

This option is incorrect because ringworm in humans is caused by a group of fungi known as dermatophytes. These fungi can infect the skin, hair, and nails, leading to the development of ringworm, also known as dermatophytosis.

This option is incorrect because ringworm in humans is caused by a group of fungi known as dermatophytes. These fungi can infect the skin, hair, and nails, leading to the development of ringworm, also known as dermatophytosis.

questions.explanation: Ringworm (tinea) is a fungal infection of the skin, the scalp, or the ails. Ringworm is caused by the dermatophyte fungi species of Microsporum, trichophyton, and epidermophyton ringworms and also affects animals, a source of infection for humans. It can be spread by direct contact or via infected materials. The lesions of ringworm may form partial or complete rings and may cause intense itching. The disease is treated with antifungal agents taken by mouth or applied locally. These fungi can infect the skin, hair, and nails, leading to the development of ringworm, also known as dermatophytosis.

questions.text: Which of the following pairs of diseases is caused by a virus?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Rabies is a viral disease that affects the central nervous system. It is caused by the rabies virus, which is typically transmitted through the saliva of infected animals, most commonly through the bite of an infected animal. Symptoms of rabies in humans can include fever, headache, weakness, and later, more severe symptoms like confusion, hallucinations, paralysis, and difficulty swallowing. Mumps is a viral infection caused by the mumps virus. It primarily affects the salivary glands, leading to painful swelling of the parotid glands in the cheeks, as well as other symptoms. Symptoms of mumps can include fever, headache, muscle aches, loss of appetite, and swelling of the salivary glands, which can lead to a characteristic "chipmunk cheek" appearance.

Cholera is an acute diarrheal disease caused by the bacterium Vibrio cholerae. It is typically transmitted through the ingestion of contaminated water or food. Cholera symptoms include severe diarrhea, vomiting, and leg cramps. The loss of fluids through diarrhea can lead to rapid dehydration and electrolyte imbalances. Cholera can be a severe and life-threatening disease if not treated promptly. Tuberculosis is a bacterial infection caused by Mycobacterium tuberculosis. It primarily affects the lungs but can also involve other parts of the body. TB symptoms can include a persistent cough, fever, night sweats, and weight loss. In pulmonary TB, patients may cough up sputum containing the bacteria. TB is contagious and is spread through the air when an infected person with active TB coughs or sneezes.

Typhoid, also known as typhoid fever, is a bacterial infection caused by Salmonella enterica serotype Typhi. It is primarily transmitted through the consumption of food or water contaminated with the bacterium. Symptoms of typhoid can include sustained high fever, headache, abdominal pain, and diarrhea. In severe cases, it can lead to complications such as intestinal perforation. Tetanus is a bacterial infection caused by the bacterium Clostridium tetani. It typically enters the body through wounds, cuts, or injuries, especially those contaminated with soil or dirt. Symptoms of tetanus can include muscle stiffness, muscle spasms, and difficulty swallowing.

AIDS is a condition caused by the human immunodeficiency virus (HIV). It primarily affects the immune system, leading to a weakened immune response and making the body more susceptible to opportunistic infections and certain cancers. Early HIV infection may not produce noticeable symptoms, but over time, without treatment, it can progress to AIDS, characterized by severe immune system impairment and the occurrence of opportunistic infections. Syphilis is a chronic sexually transmitted infection caused by Treponema pallidum. It progresses through stages—primary, secondary, latent, and tertiary—each with distinct symptoms such as painless ulcers, rashes, or systemic complications. If untreated, it can damage the heart, brain, nerves, and other organs. Early diagnosis and treatment with penicillin can completely cure the disease and prevent long-term effects.

questions.explanation: Mumps is an infectious disease that causes fever, difficulty in opening the mouth, and painful swelling of the parotid glands, which lie just below the earlobes. It is caused by a paramyxovirus, which is present in the saliva of the infected person. Rabies (hydrophobia) is caused by a virus named the rabies virus. It is introduced into the body through the bite of rabid (mad) dogs. The most important characteristic symptom of this disease is the fear of water.

questions.text: The main reason why antibodies could not solve all the problems of bacteria-mediated disease was:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: Decreased efficiency of the immune system
options.explanation: This option is incorrect, as the ability of antibodies to solve bacteria-mediated diseases is not primarily about the immune system's efficiency but rather the bacteria's ability to develop resistance.

options.text: Insensitivity of the individual following prolonged exposure to antibiotics
options.explanation: This option is incorrect because it refers to antibiotic resistance, which is different from antibody resistance. Antibiotics and antibodies function differently in combatting infections.

options.text: Development of mutant strains resistant to antibodies
options.explanation: This option is correct. Bacteria can mutate and develop strains that are resistant to antibodies, making these antibodies ineffective against such strains.

options.text: Inactivation of antibiotics by bacterial enzymes
options.explanation: This option is incorrect as it relates to antibiotics, not antibodies. Bacterial enzymes may inactivate antibiotics, but this does not apply to antibody resistance.

questions.explanation: Bacteria develop mutant strains that become resistant to antibodies, rendering these antibodies incapable of combating diseases mediated by bacteria.

questions.text: Give the correct matching of causative agent/germ and disease.
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: Anopheles is a genus of mosquitoes, and it is particularly known for its role in the transmission of malaria. Female Anopheles mosquitoes are responsible for transmitting the Plasmodium parasite, which causes malaria, when they bite humans or animals.Female Anopheles mosquitoes are vectors for the Plasmodium parasite, which causes malaria. When an infected Anopheles mosquito bites a person, it injects the parasite into the bloodstream, leading to malaria infection.Preventing malaria involves various strategies, including the use of bed nets, insect repellents, and antimalarial medications. Efforts to control the mosquito population and reduce breeding sites also play a crucial role in malaria prevention.

Leishmania and sleeping sickness are two distinct diseases caused by different parasites. Leishmania is a protozoan parasite responsible for causing a group of diseases collectively known as leishmaniasis.Leishmaniasis is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected sandflies. The disease can manifest in different forms, including cutaneous leishmaniasis, mucocutaneous leishmaniasis, and visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar).Visceral leishmaniasis, or kala-azar, is the most severe form and affects internal organs, particularly the spleen and liver. It is often fatal if left untreated.Sleeping sickness is caused by a different type of protozoan parasite, Trypanosoma brucei, and is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected tsetse flies.The disease is characterized by two stages: an early hemolymphatic stage with symptoms like fever, joint pain, and itching, and a late encephalitic stage in which the parasites invade the central nervous system, leading to neurological symptoms such as confusion, sleep disturbances, and coma.

Glossina, also known as tsetse flies, and kala-azar (visceral leishmaniasis) are two separate entities with different disease associations. Glossina, commonly referred to as tsetse flies, is a type of blood-feeding fly found in certain regions of sub-Saharan Africa.These flies are known for their role as vectors in the transmission of trypanosomes, which are parasites responsible for causing African trypanosomiasis, commonly known as sleeping sickness. There are two forms of sleeping sickness: Trypanosoma brucei gambiense and Trypanosoma brucei rhodesiense.Kala-azar, also known as visceral leishmaniasis, is a severe parasitic disease caused by protozoa of the Leishmania genus.The disease is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected sandflies, not tsetse flies. Kala-azar primarily affects internal organs, such as the spleen and liver, and is characterized by symptoms like prolonged fever, weight loss, anemia, and an enlarged abdomen.

Wuchereria bancrofti is a parasitic nematode worm that causes lymphatic filariasis, commonly known as elephantiasis. This parasitic worm is one of the primary causative agents of lymphatic filariasis, a neglected tropical disease. It primarily affects the lymphatic system in the human body.Lymphatic filariasis is characterized by the presence of adult worms in the lymphatic vessels and the accumulation of lymphatic fluid. This can lead to various symptoms, including swelling and enlargement of body parts, such as the legs and scrotum. In advanced stages, it can cause severe disfigurement and disability.In summary, Wuchereria bancrofti is a parasitic worm that causes lymphatic filariasis, a debilitating disease that affects millions of people in endemic regions. It is transmitted to humans through the bite of infected mosquitoes, and efforts are made to control its spread and provide treatment to those affected.

questions.explanation: Wuchereria bancrofti is a parasitic filarial nematode worm transmitted by a mosquito vector. It is one of the three parasites that cause lymphatic filariasis. If left untreated, it can lead to elephantiasis. Treatment options are limited, and no vaccines have been developed.Malaria is caused by protozoan parasites of the genus Plasmodium and is transmitted by female Anopheles mosquitoes.Sleeping sickness, also known as African trypanosomiasis, is a parasitic disease affecting both people and animals. It is caused by protozoa of the genus Trypanosoma and transmitted by the tsetse fly.Kala-azar, caused by the protozoan Leishmania, is transmitted by sandflies.

questions.text: Viroids differ from viruses in having:
questions.subject: Biology

options.text: DNA molecules without a protein coat.
options.explanation: Viroids do not contain DNA molecules. They consist of single-stranded RNA.

options.text: RNA molecules with a protein coat.
options.explanation: This describes viruses, not viroids. Viruses typically have a protein coat (capsid) surrounding their genetic material, which can be either DNA or RNA.

options.text: RNA molecules without a protein coat.
options.explanation: This is the correct description of viroids. Viroids are small, circular RNA molecules that lack a protein coat. They are unique infectious agents that can cause disease in plants.

options.text: DNA molecules with a protein coat.
options.explanation: This describes some viruses, but it is not a characteristic of viroids. Viroids do not contain DNA; they consist of RNA and lack a protein coat.

questions.explanation: RNA molecules without a protein coat: This is the correct description of viroids. Viroids are small, circular RNA molecules that lack a protein coat. They are unique infectious agents that can cause disease in plants.

questions.text: Which of the following statements is wrong for viroids?
questions.subject: Biology

options.explanation: This statement is correct. Viroids are known to cause infections, specifically in plants, leading to significant agricultural impacts. They are small, infectious RNA molecules that can disrupt normal plant functions.

options.text: Their RNA is of high molecular weight
options.explanation: This statement is incorrect. Viroids possess RNA that is characterized by very low molecular weight. This small size distinguishes them from viruses, which typically have larger and more complex RNA or DNA genomes.

This statement is correct. Unlike viruses, viroids do not have a protein coat or capsid. Their structure consists solely of a small, circular RNA molecule, which is a key feature that differentiates them from viruses.

This statement is correct. Viroids are indeed smaller than viruses, being some of the smallest known infectious agents, comprised of a single circular RNA molecule that is usually just a few hundred nucleotides long.

questions.explanation: "Their RNA is of high molecular weight" This statement is Incorrect. Viroids have RNA that is of very low molecular weight. In fact, one of the distinguishing features of viroids is their small size, as they consist of a single-stranded RNA molecule with a low molecular weight. This sets them apart from viruses, which typically have larger and mare complex genomes.
